lesson: Two-phase extraction (parse then resolve) is essential for cross-file references. The first phase records unresolved references as data; the second phase, after all files are seen, promotes them to edges.

In a multi-agent system without peer messaging, 'memory-as-bus + lead-orchestrated phases' is the only reliable coordination pattern. The lead reads outputs from a shared store and spawns the next agent with explicit input keys.
origin: hard-won across multiple prior agent system designs

lesson: For a code graph queried by AI agents 90% of the time, the hot path is single-symbol lookup and 1-3 hop traversal. Optimize trunk lookup and synapse adjacency for these cases; do not over-engineer deep graph algorithms.
origin: benchmark analysis of codegraph and Neo4j

lesson: AI consumers benefit massively (3-4x) from compact symbolic serialization (Emmet-style) vs JSON. Token efficiency is a first-class design constraint, not a polish item.
origin: empirical observation across AI coding workflows

lesson: When the eventual optimal data structure (radix trie) is significantly more complex than a correct-but-suboptimal one (HashMap), ship the simple version with the full test suite first. Tests pin semantics; the data-structure swap is then a pure refactor with bench deltas — the cleanest possible PR.

A materialized-path scheme MUST distinguish 'startsWith(prefix+separator)' from 'startsWith(prefix)' when computing descendants — otherwise `Auth` will incorrectly match `AuthService`. Regression test: `trunk_descendants_must_not_match_prefix_of_sibling_segment_name`.

tree-sitter impl_item in Rust grammar uses a "type" field (not "name") for the implementing type; for generic impls like Vec<T>, the type text includes angle brackets which must be stripped before using as a TrunkPath segment. Use child_by_field_name("type") and split on "<" to get the base type name.

When a test fails on CI but passes locally with no actionable error message (e.g. bare 'assertion failed: status.success()'), the immediate fix is NOT to guess the root cause — it's to add stderr/stdout capture and re-run. One CI cycle with diagnostics beats five cycles of speculation. Pattern: change .status() to .output() and include stderr in the assertion message; the next run will show 'exit=-1073741571' or similar, which is enough to root-cause.

For sequential releases (v0.1.4 then v0.1.5), the cleanest gitflow is: cut release/v0.1.4 → ship → back-merge to develop → cut release/v0.1.5 from now-updated develop. Skipping the back-merge step (and cutting v0.1.5 from stale develop) means v0.1.5's CHANGELOG inherits a broken [Unreleased] section that mixes v0.1.4 and v0.1.5 work, requiring manual restructuring at release-cut time.

For tests that assert on file counts or layout, prefer assertions on KNOWN-FILE EXISTENCE over count thresholds. 'store.lookup("crates/mycelium-core/src/lib.rs").is_some()' is robust across all checkout / vendor / package layouts; 'files_indexed >= 100' is fragile and breaks on CI's cargo package layout. The count assertion catches the same class of bug (walker missing a category of files) without being layout-dependent.

GitHub auto-delete-head-branch setting deletes release/* branches when a PR targeting main is closed (even if not merged). This makes back-merge PRs impossible via GitHub MCP (422 head Code:invalid). Prevention: (a) disable auto-delete for release/* branches in repo settings, OR (b) recreate from tag when needed using `git fetch origin refs/tags/vX.Y.Z:refs/tags/vX.Y.Z && git checkout -b release/vX.Y.Z vX.Y.Z && git push -u origin release/vX.Y.Z`. The tag always survives.
